---
title: "Your Title"
subtitle: "BMIN503/EPID600 Final Project"
author: "FirstName LastName"
format: html
editor: visual
number-sections: true
embed-resources: true
---

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Use this template to complete your project throughout the course. Your Final Project presentation will be based on the contents of this document. Replace the title/name above and text below with your own, but keep the headers. Feel free to change the theme and other display settings, although this is not required.

## Overview {#sec-overview}

Give a brief a description of your project and its goal(s), what data you are using to complete it, and what two faculty/staff in different fields you have spoken to about your project with a brief summary of what you learned from each person. Include a link to your final project GitHub repository.

The aim of this project is to develop a predictive model for mental health appointment utilization. The data used in this project comes from Epic Analytics of outpatient mental health visits in Penn Medicine Outpatient Psychiatry Clinic (OPC) in the last year. I consulted with Dr. Theodore Satterthwaithe, Director of Penn Lifespan Informatics and Neuroimaging Center (PennLINC), who helped me narrow down the scope of this project. Additionally, I discussed the potential operational impact of this project with Dr. Olga Barg, Director of Clinical Informatics for the Department of Psychiatry.

[lescanico/BMIN503_Final_Project: Template repo for Data Science for Biomedical Informatics (BMIN503/EPID600) final project (github.com)](https://github.com/lescanico/BMIN503_Final_Project)

## Introduction {#sec-introduction}

Describe the problem addressed, its significance, and some background to motivate the problem. This should extend what is in the @sec-overview.

Mental health service utilization is a critical area in healthcare management. Resource allocation, including scheduling and provider assignment, is key to ensuring that patients receive timely care. The complexity of mental health conditions makes it challenging to match patients with the appropriate provider type (psychiatrist, NP, resident) and to predict future appointment needs. This project addresses the problem by developing a machine learning model that predicts key factors influencing appointment utilization: frequency, duration, provider type, visit type, and likelihood of attendance.

Explain why your problem is interdisciplinary, what fields can contribute to its understanding, and incorporate background related to what you learned from meeting with faculty/staff.

The problem is interdisciplinary as it integrates psychiatry, healthcare management, and data science. Psychiatry helps define patient care needs, healthcare management provides operational context, and data science tools facilitate the analysis and modeling of historical data to make predictions.

## Methods {#sec-methods}

Describe the data used and general methodological approach used to address the problem described in the @sec-introduction. Subsequently, incorporate full R code necessary to retrieve and clean data, and perform analysis. Be sure to include a description of code so that others (including your future self) can understand what you are doing and why.

[**Data**]{.underline}

The dataset consists of various fields related to demographics, clinical characteristics, and appointment data.

Some of the key variables used in this project include:

\- **Primary Provider Type**: Indicates whether the provider is a psychiatrist, NP, or resident.

\- **Age**: Patient's age at the time of visit.

\- **Appointment Frequency**: The number of appointments a patient has attended.

\- **No-show History**: Whether the patient missed previous appointments.

\- **Copay Information**: Indicates financial barriers, which could affect attendance.

[**Data Cleaning and Preparation**]{.underline}

```{r}
# Load necessary libraries
library(tidyverse)
library(randomForest)

# Set 
setwd("~/GitHub/BMIN503_Final_Project")

# Load data
data <- read.csv('OPC_last_year_visits.csv')

# Clean data - remove missing values, convert relevant fields
data_clean <- data %>%
  filter(!is.na(Primary.Provider.Type)) %>%
  mutate(Age = as.numeric(Age))

# Train-test split
set.seed(123)
train_indices <- sample(seq_len(nrow(data_clean)), size = 0.8 * nrow(data_clean))
train_data <- data_clean[train_indices, ]
test_data <- data_clean[-train_indices, ]
```

### Model Building

For prediction, we use a **Random Forest model** due to its robustness in handling large datasets and identifying variable importance. The following R code demonstrates the training of the model.

```         
# Build Random Forest Model
model <- randomForest(Appointment.Frequency ~ Age + Primary.Provider.Type + No.show.History + Copay.Due,
                      data = train_data, importance = TRUE)

# Predict on test data
predicted <- predict(model, test_data)
```

## Results {#sec-results}

Describe your results and include relevant tables, plots, and code/comments used to obtain them. You may refer to the @sec-methods as needed. End with a brief conclusion of your findings related to the question you set out to address. You can include references if you'd like, but this is not required.

## Conclusion

This the conclusion. The @sec-results can be invoked here.
